1: Machir, the firstborn of Manasseh, is given Gilead and Bashan (the trans-Jordan portion). The identification of Machir as a 'man of war' who had already taken Gilead explains why his descendants received the less-settled trans-Jordan territory — they were equipped militarily for it. Machir's descendants later appear as Mephibosheth's protectors (2 Samuel 9:4-5; Joshua 17:27), maintaining a trans-Jordan presence into the Davidic period.
2: The Cisjordan Manassites are distributed by clan: Abiezer, Helek, Asriel, Shechem, Hepher, Shemida. These clan names appear in the Samaria Ostraca (eighth century BCE administrative dockets from the northern kingdom), confirming that these were real administrative divisions in the later Israelite state — the allotment's clan-level organization persisted for centuries into the monarchy.
3: Zelophehad's daughters — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah — invoke a legal precedent already established in Numbers 27:1-11 and ratified with additional conditions in Numbers 36:1-12. The original case arose from Zelophehad's death without male heirs; YHWH's ruling (Numbers 27:7-8) established women's inheritance rights when there are no sons. The application in Joshua shows the allotment process operating under formal case law — the daughters' claim is not a novelty but the application of binding legal precedent.
4: 'They appeared before Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the leaders' — the formal judicial forum, the same three-party body that oversees all the allotments (Joshua 14:1; 19:51). The daughters' citation of the specific YHWH-to-Moses ruling (Numbers 27:7) demonstrates legal literacy: they know the applicable law and cite it correctly. Their inheritance was granted 'as the LORD commanded Moses' — the allotment process is a judicial one where legal precedent is authoritative.
5: Manasseh received ten portions: five male clan portions (Joshua 17:2) plus five female portions from Zelophehad's five daughters. This arithmetic is unusual — normally a tribe receives one block of territory — and reflects the complexity of the Manasseh clan structure (split between trans-Jordan and Cisjordan; daughters inheriting alongside sons). The number ten may also reflect a fuller administrative list not fully preserved in Joshua 17:1-6.
6: 'Manasseh's daughters inherited among his sons' — a formal statement affirming the daughters' full inheritance status alongside male heirs. This was not the cultural default in the ancient Near East (Mesopotamian law codes generally favored male heirs, with daughters receiving dowry instead of inheritance); the Israelite case law represented in Num 27 and applied here was distinctive in recognizing daughters' inheritance claims when no sons existed.
7: Manasseh's western boundary runs from Asher to Michmethath (facing Shechem). Shechem is the chapter's geographic anchor — the most important city in Manasseh's territory, already the site of Jacob's altar (Genesis 33:18-20), Joseph's bones burial (Joshua 24:32), and the covenant renewal (Joshua 24:1). Its position at the center of the boundary description reflects its central role in Israelite religious and political life.
8: Tappuah — the city belonged to Manasseh but the land of Tappuah on Manasseh's border belonged to Ephraim. This administrative split (city in one tribe, surrounding agricultural land in another) reflects the complexity of settlement-era land tenure. Cities and their agricultural hinterlands were not always administered as a unit; the allotment process distinguished between the fortified urban center and the surrounding fields and villages.
9: The Kanah Ravine (same as ch.Joshua 16:8) separates Ephraim from Manasseh; Ephraimite cities north of the ravine within Manasseh's territory again reflect the settlement intermingling noted in Joshua 16:9. The Kanah/Wadi Qana was a real geographic boundary used consistently in both tribal descriptions — confirmation that these chapters derive from actual survey data.
10: Ephraim to the south, Manasseh to the north, the sea on the west — the basic tribal geography of the central highlands. Their junction at Asher and Issachar to the north sets up the transition to the northern allotments in Joshua 19. The central highlands were the Joseph tribes' heartland; the northern valleys (Jezreel, Beth-shean) remained Canaanite (Joshua 17:11-12), separating the central hill country from the Galilee tribes.
11: Manasseh's possession within Issachar and Asher: Beth-shean, Ibleam, Dor, En-dor, Taanach, Megiddo — and their 'three heights' (or 'Naphoth-dor'). This is the most strategically important city list in the allotment chapters: Beth-shean controlled the Jordan valley / Jezreel junction; Megiddo controlled the Carmel pass into the Jezreel Valley; Taanach guarded the same approaches. All six cities — the major fortresses of the Jezreel and Beth-shean valleys — are listed as Manasseh's possession and immediately noted (Joshua 17:12) as unconquered.
12: 'The Manassites were unable to drive out the people of those towns' — the failure notice for the entire Jezreel-Beth-shean valley zone. The archaeological record confirms continued Canaanite occupation at Beth-shean (Egyptian-administered through the Late Bronze Age), Megiddo, and Taanach throughout Iron Age I. The 'inability' was not merely military reluctance but a real technological disparity: Canaanite chariot forces in the flat valleys were strategically superior to Israelite infantry.
13: 'When the Israelites grew stronger, they subjected the Canaanites to forced labor but did not drive them out completely.' The trajectory — military incapacity → political domination via labor conscription — is identical to Ephraim's arrangement at Gezer (Joshua 16:10). Forced labor (mas) became the characteristic Israelite response to unconquerable Canaanite cities; it became the structural model for the Solomonic corvée that eventually provoked the northern secession (1 Kings 5:13-14; Joshua 12:4,18).
14: 'Why have you given us only one allotment and one portion? We are a numerous people.' The Joseph tribe's complaint reveals several tensions in the allotment process: (1) the lot-based system gave equal weight to all tribes regardless of population; (2) the Joseph tribes believed their demographic size entitled them to more territory; (3) the complaint implies dissatisfaction with what they have received — a contrast with Caleb's eagerness to take the hardest territory (ch.Joshua 14:12). The social ethic of the allotment is egalitarian; the complaint reflects a different value system.
15: Joshua's reply: 'if you are such a great people, go up into the forest and clear land there in the territory of the Perizzites and Rephaim.' The 'forest' (hayyāʿar) in the central highlands of Ephraim and Manasseh is historically attested by pollen analysis: Iron Age I sites in the central hill country show significant deforestation, confirming that early Israelite settlers were literally clearing forest for terraced agriculture. Joshua's challenge is not rhetorical but practical — the unheld territory was wooded highland, not flat farmland. The Perizzites (forest-dwellers?) and Rephaim (giants) still needed clearing.
16: 'The hill country is not enough, and all the Canaanites in the lowland have iron chariots.' Iron chariots (rekeb barzel) represent the decisive military technology of the Late Bronze / early Iron Age transition. Iron-fitted chariot wheels and fittings gave Canaanite forces in the flat valleys (Jezreel, Beth-shean) an overwhelming tactical advantage over Israelite infantry — iron could not be effectively worked by Israelites until after the Philistine period (1 Samuel 13:19-22). The complaint is militarily honest: the hill country can be settled, but the valley floor cannot be taken by force until Israel achieves iron-working parity.
17: Joshua's counter: 'you are a great people and have great power — you will not have just one allotment.' The argument inverts the complaint: their greatness is the reason they can clear more territory, not the reason they deserve more granted territory. The distinction between claimed territory and cleared-and-settled territory is fundamental to the allotment theology: YHWH gives the title; Israel does the work of possession. Joshua's challenge is covenantal — great resources mean great responsibility to act on what has been given.
18: 'The forested hill country will be yours; clear it, and it will be yours to its farthest limits.' This verse encapsulates the allotment's underlying principle: the land is given, but possession requires human effort and covenant obedience. The Canaanite iron chariots in the valley are not dismissed — 'you shall drive them out even though they have iron chariots and are strong.' The promise is not of an easy victory but of a certain one, contingent on Israel's engagement. The failure to take the valley cities (Joshua 17:12-13) was a failure of will and covenant obedience, not a divine retraction of the promise.
